title: Modular inverse (prime modulus)
description: Find a^(-1) mod p using Fermat's little theorem

approaches.name: Brute Force O(p)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers from 1 to p-1 to find a value x such that (a * x) % p == 1. This is straightforward but inefficient for large p.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Modular Inverse
ladders.desc: Find a^(-1) mod p by checking all numbers from 1 to p-1 until finding x where (a*x) % p == 1.
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse of a modulo p (where p is prime) is an integer x such that (a*x) ≡ 1 (mod p). This means when you multiply a by x and take the remainder when divided by p, you get 1. The brute force approach systematically tests every possible candidate x from 1 to p-1. This works because the modular inverse must exist (since p is prime and a is not divisible by p) and must be in the range [1, p-1]. The algorithm iterates through this range, checking each candidate using the modulo operation. If no inverse is found (which shouldn't happen with valid inputs), we return -1 or indicate failure. The intuition is straightforward: since the inverse exists somewhere in that finite set, we can find it by exhaustive search. Time complexity is O(p) because we might check all p-1 numbers in the worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) which is the iteration and checking logic. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (a=0, p=2), forgetting that 0 has no inverse, or using the wrong range. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse_brute(a, p): for x in range(1, p): if (a*x) % p == 1: return x return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Candidate Checker
ladders.desc: Iterate from 1 to p-1 and check if (a*x) % p equals 1.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. It takes integers a and p, loops through x from 1 to p-1, and for each x, computes (a*x) % p. If this equals 1, it returns x as the modular inverse. If the loop completes without finding such an x, it returns -1 to indicate no inverse exists (though for prime p and a not divisible by p, this shouldn't happen). The intuition is that we're performing a linear search over a finite solution space. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. For x from 1 to p-1: 2. Compute product = a * x. 3. Compute remainder = product % p. 4. If remainder == 1, return x. 5. After loop, return -1. Time complexity is O(p) for the loop, with O(1) operations per iteration. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the complete solution when combined with input validation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range, integer overflow for large a and x (though in Python it's fine), or not handling the case where a=0. Real code pattern: def find_inverse_iterative(a, p): for x in range(1, p): if (a * x) % p == 1: return x return -1.

examples.trace: Note: p=9 is not prime, so inverse may not exist. x=1: 4%9=4 ≠1. x=2: 8 ≠1. x=3: 12%9=3 ≠1. x=4: 16%9=7 ≠1. x=5: 20%9=2 ≠1. x=6: 24%9=6 ≠1. x=7: 28%9=1 ✓ Wait, 4*7=28, 28%9=1, so output should be 7. Correction: p=9 is not prime, but inverse might exist. Let's use a=4, p=6 (non-prime where inverse doesn't exist). x=1: 4%6=4 ≠1. x=2: 8%6=2 ≠1. x=3: 12%6=0 ≠1. x=4: 16%6=4 ≠1. x=5: 20%6=2 ≠1. Return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Modular Congruence Check
ladders.desc: Check if (a*x) % p == 1 for given a, x, and p.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental check that determines if x is the modular inverse of a modulo p. The condition (a*x) % p == 1 means that when a is multiplied by x, the product leaves a remainder of 1 when divided by p. In modular arithmetic terms, a*x ≡ 1 (mod p). The intuition is that we're verifying a candidate solution by performing the multiplication and modulo operations. The step-by-step process: 1. Multiply a and x to get product. 2. Compute product modulo p to get remainder. 3. Compare remainder with 1. If equal, x is the inverse; otherwise, it's not. Time complexity is O(1) for the arithmetic operations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 as the condition checked in each iteration of the loop. Common mistakes include using integer division instead of modulo, or misunderstanding that we need the remainder to be exactly 1, not just that the product is congruent to 1 mod p (which is the same thing). Real code pattern: def is_inverse(a, x, p): return (a * x) % p == 1.

examples.trace: Compute product = 3*2 = 6. Compute remainder = 6 % 5 = 1. Check 1 == 1 → True.
Product = 4*3 = 12. Remainder = 12 % 7 = 5. Check 5 == 1 → False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted as a % p in programming, returns the remainder when a is divided by p. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 divided by 3 gives quotient 2 and remainder 1. Formally, a % p = a - p * floor(a/p). The intuition is that it wraps numbers around a cycle of length p, which is essential in modular arithmetic. In the context of finding modular inverses, we use modulo to check if (a*x) is congruent to 1 mod p. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Divide a by p to get quotient q = floor(a/p). 2. Compute remainder r = a - p*q. 3. Ensure r is in the range [0, p-1]. Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the operation used to compute the remainder in the congruence check. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, or not handling negative numbers correctly (in Python, a % p always returns a non-negative result). Real code pattern: def mod(a, p): return a % p.

examples.trace: 10 divided by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 10 - 3*3 = 10 - 9 = 1.
15 divided by 4: quotient = 3, remainder = 15 - 4*3 = 15 - 12 = 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Multiplication
ladders.desc: Multiply two integers to get their product.
ladders.explanation: Integer multiplication is the arithmetic operation of scaling one number by another. For integers a and x, the product is a*x, which represents adding a to itself x times. In the context of modular inverses, we multiply a by a candidate x to check if the product is congruent to 1 modulo p. The intuition is straightforward: we're combining the two numbers through multiplication. The step-by-step algorithm for small numbers: repeated addition. For larger numbers, use standard multiplication algorithms. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers, but can be O(n^2) for large arbitrary-precision numbers. Space complexity is O(1) for fixed-size, O(n) for arbitrary-precision. This feeds into L3 as part of computing a*x before taking the modulo. Common mistakes include integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers (not an issue in Python), or confusing multiplication with other operations. Real code pattern: def multiply(a, x): return a * x.

examples.trace: 3 * 4 = 12. Alternatively, 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Variables and Loops
ladders.desc: Use variables to store integers and loops to repeat operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves using integer variables to store numerical values and loops to perform repetitive tasks. In programming, variables like a, p, and x hold integer values. A loop (e.g., a for-loop) allows us to execute a block of code multiple times, such as checking each candidate x from 1 to p-1. The intuition is that we need to automate repeated checks without writing separate code for each value. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize variables with input values. 2. Set up a loop that iterates x from 1 to p-1. 3. Inside the loop, perform operations using the current x. Time complexity depends on the loop iterations; here, up to O(p). Space complexity is O(1) for the variables. This feeds into L4 by providing the structure to repeatedly multiply and check. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors), not updating loop variables, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: for x in range(1, p): # do something with x.

examples.trace: Initialize loop with x=1. Execute loop body. Increment x to 2, execute. Continue until x=4, execute. When x becomes 5, it's not less than 5, so loop stops.
x=1: execute. x=2: execute. x=3: not less than 3, stop.

approaches.name: Fermat's Little Theorem with Fast Exponentiation O(log p)
approaches.summary: Use Fermat's little theorem: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 mod p, so a^(-1) ≡ a^(p-2) mod p. Compute a^(p-2) mod p using fast modular exponentiation (binary exponentiation). This is efficient and leverages prime modulus properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Modular Inverse Using Fermat's Little Theorem
ladders.desc: Compute a^(-1) mod p for prime p using a^(p-2) mod p via fast exponentiation.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the modular inverse of a modulo p, where p is prime. Fermat's Little Theorem states that for prime p and integer a not divisible by p, a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). Rearranging gives a^(p-2) ≡ a^(-1) (mod p). The challenge is computing a^(p-2) mod p efficiently when p can be large (e.g., up to 10^9+7). Direct computation would be O(p), which is infeasible. Instead, we use binary exponentiation (fast exponentiation) to compute this in O(log p) time. The algorithm works by expressing the exponent p-2 in binary, then squaring the base and multiplying conditionally. For example, to compute 3^13 mod 11, we break 13 into binary (1101) and compute step-by-step: start with result=1, base=3, exponent=13. While exponent>0: if exponent is odd, multiply result by base mod 11; square base mod 11; halve exponent. This yields 3^13 mod 11 = 9, so 3^(-1) mod 11 = 9. The intuition is that exponentiation by squaring reduces multiplications from linear to logarithmic. Time complexity is O(log p) due to the exponent's bits; space is O(1) as we use a few variables. Connection to L1: This directly uses the fast modular exponentiation subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling a=0 (inverse doesn't exist), not using modulo operations to prevent overflow, and incorrect binary exponentiation logic. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, p): if a % p == 0: return None; return pow(a, p-2, p) using Python's built-in, or implement binary exponentiation manually.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check a % p = 3 % 11 = 3 ≠ 0, so inverse exists. Step 2: Compute a^(p-2) mod p = 3^(11-2) mod 11 = 3^9 mod 11. Step 3: Binary exponentiation: exponent = 9 (binary 1001). Initialize result = 1, base = 3. While exponent > 0: exponent=9 (odd): result = (1 * 3) mod 11 = 3, base = (3^2) mod 11 = 9, exponent = 4; exponent=4 (even): result=3, base = (9^2) mod 11 = 81 mod 11 = 4, exponent=2; exponent=2 (even): result=3, base = (4^2) mod 11 = 16 mod 11 = 5, exponent=1; exponent=1 (odd): result = (3 * 5) mod 11 = 15 mod 11 = 4, base = (5^2) mod 11 = 25 mod 11 = 3, exponent=0. Final result = 4. Thus, 3^(-1) mod 11 = 4. Wait, check: 3 * 4 = 12 mod 11 = 1, correct.

Step 1: a % p = 5 % 13 = 5 ≠ 0. Step 2: Compute 5^(13-2) mod 13 = 5^11 mod 13. Binary exponentiation: exponent=11 (binary 1011). result=1, base=5. Loop: exponent=11 odd: result=(1*5) mod 13=5, base=(5^2) mod 13=25 mod 13=12, exponent=5; exponent=5 odd: result=(5*12) mod 13=60 mod 13=8 (since 13*4=52, 60-52=8), base=(12^2) mod 13=144 mod 13=1 (13*11=143, 144-143=1), exponent=2; exponent=2 even: result=8, base=(1^2) mod 13=1, exponent=1; exponent=1 odd: result=(8*1) mod 13=8, base=(1^2) mod 13=1, exponent=0. Result=8. Check: 5 * 8 = 40 mod 13 = 1 (13*3=39), correct.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Fast Modular Exponentiation (Binary Exponentiation)
ladders.desc: Compute a^b mod m efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes a^b mod m in O(log b) time, which is crucial for the modular inverse problem where b = p-2 can be large. The intuition is to break the exponent b into its binary representation, allowing us to square the base repeatedly and multiply only when a bit is set. For example, to compute 3^13 mod 11, note that 13 in binary is 1101, which means 3^13 = 3^(8+4+1) = 3^8 * 3^4 * 3^1. Instead of computing each power separately, we build them iteratively: start with result=1, base=3. For each bit from LSB to MSB: if current bit is 1, multiply result by base mod m; then square base mod m for the next bit. Algorithm: function fast_pow(a, b, m): result = 1; while b > 0: if b % 2 == 1: result = (result * a) % m; a = (a * a) % m; b = b // 2; return result. Time complexity is O(log b) because b halves each iteration; space is O(1). Connection to L0: This is the core computation for a^(p-2) mod p. Common mistakes include forgetting modulo operations during multiplication (leading to overflow), incorrect handling of b=0, and off-by-one errors in the loop. Real code pattern: def pow_mod(a, b, m): res=1; while b: if b&1: res=(res*a)%m; a=(a*a)%m; b>>=1; return res.

examples.trace: Compute 3^5 mod 7. b=5 (binary 101). result=1, base=3, m=7. Loop: b=5 odd: result=(1*3) mod 7=3, base=(3^2) mod 7=9 mod 7=2, b=2; b=2 even: result=3, base=(2^2) mod 7=4, b=1; b=1 odd: result=(3*4) mod 7=12 mod 7=5, base=(4^2) mod 7=16 mod 7=2, b=0. Return result=5. Check: 3^5=243, 243 mod 7 = 5 (7*34=238, 243-238=5).

Compute 2^10 mod 11. b=10 (binary 1010). result=1, base=2, m=11. Loop: b=10 even: result=1, base=(2^2) mod 11=4, b=5; b=5 odd: result=(1*4) mod 11=4, base=(4^2) mod 11=16 mod 11=5, b=2; b=2 even: result=4, base=(5^2) mod 11=25 mod 11=3, b=1; b=1 odd: result=(4*3) mod 11=12 mod 11=1, base=(3^2) mod 11=9, b=0. Return result=1. Check: 2^10=1024, 1024 mod 11 = 1 (11*93=1023).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Fermat's Little Theorem Application
ladders.desc: Derive a^(-1) mod p = a^(p-2) mod p using Fermat's theorem for prime p.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight that enables computing modular inverses efficiently for prime moduli. Fermat's Little Theorem states: if p is prime and a is an integer not divisible by p, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). The intuition is that in modulo p arithmetic, multiplying a by all non-zero elements cycles back to 1 after p-1 steps. To find the inverse a^(-1) such that a * a^(-1) ≡ 1 (mod p), we rearrange the theorem: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 => a * a^(p-2) ≡ 1 (mod p). Thus, a^(p-2) mod p is the inverse. For example, for a=3, p=5: 3^(5-1)=3^4=81 ≡ 1 mod 5, so 3^(5-2)=3^3=27 ≡ 2 mod 5, and indeed 3*2=6 ≡ 1 mod 5. This reduces the inverse problem to exponentiation. The algorithm is straightforward: given a and prime p, if a % p == 0, no inverse; else compute a^(p-2) mod p. Time complexity depends on the exponentiation method; with fast exponentiation, it's O(log p). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic specifies the exponent b = p-2 for the fast exponentiation subroutine. Common mistakes include applying the theorem to non-prime p, not checking for a=0, and misunderstanding the exponent as p-1 instead of p-2. Real code pattern: if a % p == 0: return None; else: return pow(a, p-2, p).

examples.trace: Step 1: Check a % p = 2 % 7 = 2 ≠ 0, so inverse exists. Step 2: Apply Fermat's theorem: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 mod p => 2^6 ≡ 1 mod 7. Step 3: Compute inverse as a^(p-2) mod p = 2^(7-2) mod 7 = 2^5 mod 7. Step 4: Calculate 2^5 = 32, 32 mod 7 = 4 (since 7*4=28, 32-28=4). Thus, 2^(-1) mod 7 = 4. Check: 2 * 4 = 8 ≡ 1 mod 7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Perform addition, multiplication, and exponentiation under modulo with properties like (a*b) mod m = [(a mod m)*(b mod m)] mod m.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations in modular arithmetic needed for the inverse computation. Modular arithmetic works with remainders: a mod m is the remainder when a is divided by m. Key properties: (a + b) mod m = [(a mod m) + (b mod m)] mod m, (a * b) mod m = [(a mod m) * (b mod m)] mod m, and (a^b) mod m can be computed using repeated multiplication with modulo at each step to avoid large numbers. The intuition is that we can reduce numbers modulo m at any step without affecting the final result modulo m, which is crucial for efficiency. For example, to compute (7 * 8) mod 5, instead of 56 mod 5 = 1, we do (7 mod 5=2) * (8 mod 5=3) = 6, then 6 mod 5 = 1. For exponentiation, like 3^4 mod 7: compute stepwise: 3^1 mod 7=3, 3^2=9 mod 7=2, 3^3=(2*3) mod 7=6, 3^4=(6*3) mod 7=18 mod 7=4. Algorithm for modular multiplication: def mod_mul(a, b, m): return ((a % m) * (b % m)) % m. For modular exponentiation, use a loop: result=1; for i in range(b): result = (result * a) % m (but this is O(b), not efficient—efficiency is covered in L1). Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) per operation; space is O(1). Connection to L2: These operations are used in computing a^(p-2) mod p. Common mistakes include not applying modulo consistently, leading to integer overflow, or misunderstanding that (a mod m) * (b mod m) might still overflow if m is large—use long integers or additional modulo. Real code pattern: In Python, use % operator directly.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute directly: 12 * 15 = 180, 180 mod 7: 7*25=175, remainder 180-175=5. Step 2: Using modular property: 12 mod 7 = 5, 15 mod 7 = 1, then (5 * 1) mod 7 = 5 mod 7 = 5. Both methods yield 5.

Step 1: Start with result = 1. Step 2: Multiply by 2: result = (1 * 2) mod 11 = 2. Step 3: Multiply by 2: result = (2 * 2) mod 11 = 4. Step 4: Multiply by 2: result = (4 * 2) mod 11 = 8. Step 5: Multiply by 2: result = (8 * 2) mod 11 = 16 mod 11 = 5. Step 6: Multiply by 2: result = (5 * 2) mod 11 = 10. Thus, 2^5 mod 11 = 10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Compute a^b for integers a and b using repeated multiplication.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental concept of exponentiation: raising a number a to the power b, defined as multiplying a by itself b times. For example, 3^4 = 3 * 3 * 3 * 3 = 81. The intuition is that exponentiation is repeated multiplication; when b=0, a^0 = 1 for any a ≠ 0 (and often defined as 1 for a=0 as well). The algorithm is straightforward: initialize result = 1; for i from 1 to b: result = result * a. For negative exponents, it involves reciprocals, but here we focus on non-negative b. Time complexity is O(b) because we perform b multiplications; space is O(1) as we use a single variable. Connection to L3: This is the basis for modular exponentiation, where we add modulo operations. Common mistakes include not handling b=0 correctly, integer overflow for large results, and confusion between exponentiation and multiplication (e.g., a^2 vs a*2). Real code pattern: def pow_int(a, b): result=1; for _ in range(b): result *= a; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = 1. Step 2: Multiply by a=2: result = 1 * 2 = 2. Step 3: Multiply by 2: result = 2 * 2 = 4. Step 4: Multiply by 2: result = 4 * 2 = 8. Step 5: Multiply by 2: result = 8 * 2 = 16. Step 6: Multiply by 2: result = 16 * 2 = 32. Since b=5, we did 5 multiplications. Thus, 2^5 = 32.
Step 1: result = 1. Step 2: Multiply by 5: result = 1 * 5 = 5. Step 3: Multiply by 5: result = 5 * 5 = 25. Step 4: Multiply by 5: result = 25 * 5 = 125. Thus, 5^3 = 125.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Multiplication of Integers
ladders.desc: Multiply two integers using repeated addition or direct multiplication.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: multiplying integers. Multiplication is defined as repeated addition: a * b means adding a to itself b times. For example, 3 * 4 = 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12. The intuition is that multiplication scales one number by another. In programming, we use the * operator, but understanding the underlying process helps with exponentiation, which is repeated multiplication. The algorithm can be implemented as: function multiply(a, b): result = 0; for i from 1 to b: result = result + a. This is O(b) time; for efficiency, we use built-in multiplication. Space is O(1). Connection to L4: Exponentiation builds on multiplication, as a^b involves multiplying a repeatedly. Common mistakes include confusing multiplication with exponentiation, or not handling negative numbers (but here we focus on non-negative). Real code pattern: In Python, use a * b directly.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with result = 0. Step 2: Add a=5: result = 0 + 5 = 5. Step 3: Add 5: result = 5 + 5 = 10. Step 4: Add 5: result = 10 + 5 = 15. Since b=3, we added 5 three times. Thus, 5 * 3 = 15.

Step 1: result = 0. Step 2: Add 2: result = 0 + 2 = 2. Step 3: Add 2: result = 2 + 2 = 4. Step 4: Add 2: result = 4 + 2 = 6. Step 5: Add 2: result = 6 + 2 = 8. Step 6: Add 2: result = 8 + 2 = 10. Step 7: Add 2: result = 10 + 2 = 12. Thus, 2 * 6 = 12.

approaches.name: Extended Euclidean Algorithm O(log min(a, p))
approaches.summary: Solve the equation a*x + p*y = 1 using the extended Euclidean algorithm to find x mod p as the modular inverse. This works for any modulus where a and p are coprime, not just prime p.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Modular Inverse Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Given integers a and p (p > 1), find a^(-1) mod p where a and p are coprime.
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse of a modulo p is an integer x such that (a * x) mod p = 1. This exists only when a and p are coprime (gcd(a, p) = 1). The Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA) solves the Bézout's identity: a*x + p*y = gcd(a, p). When gcd(a, p) = 1, this becomes a*x + p*y = 1. Taking modulo p, we get a*x ≡ 1 (mod p), so x mod p is the modular inverse. The algorithm recursively computes gcd while tracking coefficients (x, y) that satisfy the identity. Time complexity is O(log min(a, p)) due to the Euclidean algorithm's logarithmic reduction. Space is O(1) for iterative implementation. Connection: This directly solves the problem by finding x such that a*x ≡ 1 mod p. Common mistakes: Not handling negative x (adjust by adding p), assuming p is prime (works for any coprime pair), and integer overflow (use appropriate data types). Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, p): if gcd(a, p) != 1: return None; x, y = extended_gcd(a, p); return x % p.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(3,11) via EEA: Start: a=3, p=11. Step1: 11 = 3*3 + 2 → q=3, r=2. Step2: 3 = 2*1 + 1 → q=1, r=1. Step3: 2 = 1*2 + 0 → q=2, r=0. Back-substitute for coefficients: From step2: 1 = 3 - 2*1. From step1: 2 = 11 - 3*3. Substitute: 1 = 3 - (11 - 3*3)*1 = 3 - 11 + 3*3 = 4*3 - 1*11. So x=4, y=-1. x mod 11 = 4. Check: 3*4 = 12, 12 mod 11 = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extended Euclidean Algorithm for Bézout Coefficients
ladders.desc: Given integers a and b, find integers x and y such that a*x + b*y = gcd(a, b).
ladders.explanation: The Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA) extends the Euclidean algorithm to compute not only gcd(a, b) but also coefficients x and y (Bézout coefficients) that satisfy Bézout's identity: a*x + b*y = gcd(a, b). Intuition: The Euclidean algorithm computes gcd by repeated division: a = b*q + r. We can express r as a linear combination of a and b: r = a - b*q. Recursively, we back-substitute to express gcd as such a combination. Algorithm: Use recursion or iteration. For iterative: Initialize x0=1, y0=0 (for a), x1=0, y1=1 (for b). While b ≠ 0: q = a // b; (a, b) = (b, a % b); (x0, x1) = (x1, x0 - q*x1); (y0, y1) = (y1, y0 - q*y1). At end, gcd=a, x=x0, y=y0. Time: O(log min(a, b)) as each step reduces numbers exponentially. Space: O(1) iterative. Connection: This sub-routine provides x and y needed in L0 to find modular inverse. Common mistakes: Swapping coefficients, incorrect update formulas, not handling negative numbers properly. Real code pattern: def extended_gcd(a, b): if b==0: return a,1,0; gcd,x1,y1 = extended_gcd(b, a%b); x = y1; y = x1 - (a//b)*y1; return gcd,x,y.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bézout's Identity and Modular Inverse Derivation
ladders.desc: Understand how a*x + p*y = 1 leads to a^(-1) ≡ x mod p when gcd(a, p)=1.
ladders.explanation: Bézout's identity states that for integers a and p, there exist integers x and y such that a*x + p*y = gcd(a, p). When a and p are coprime, gcd(a, p)=1, so the equation becomes a*x + p*y = 1. Taking modulo p on both sides: (a*x + p*y) mod p = 1 mod p. Since p*y mod p = 0, this simplifies to a*x mod p = 1. By definition, x mod p is the modular inverse of a modulo p, denoted a^(-1) mod p. Intuition: The inverse x 'undoes' multiplication by a modulo p. The EEA finds x and y, and we take x modulo p to get the inverse in range [0, p-1]. Time: Derivation is O(1), but relies on EEA computation. Space: O(1). Connection: This logic connects EEA (L1) to the modular inverse problem (L0). Common mistakes: Forgetting to take x mod p (x might be negative), not checking gcd=1 first. Real code pattern: After EEA, if gcd!=1: no inverse; else: inverse = x % p.

examples.input: From EEA: a=3, p=11 gives x=4, y=-1
examples.trace: Given Bézout: 3*4 + 11*(-1) = 12 - 11 = 1. Since gcd(3,11)=1, we have 3*4 + 11*(-1) = 1. Take modulo 11: (3*4) mod 11 + (11*(-1)) mod 11 = 1 mod 11. 11*(-1) mod 11 = 0. So 3*4 mod 11 = 1. Thus, 4 is the inverse of 3 modulo 11. Check: 3*4=12, 12 mod 11=1.

examples.input: From EEA: a=10, p=17 gives x=-5, y=3
examples.trace: Given Bézout: 10*(-5) + 17*3 = -50 + 51 = 1. Since gcd(10,17)=1, we have 10*(-5) + 17*3 = 1. Take modulo 17: (10*(-5)) mod 17 + (17*3) mod 17 = 1 mod 17. 17*3 mod 17 = 0. So 10*(-5) mod 17 = 1. Compute -5 mod 17: -5 + 17 = 12. Thus, 12 is the inverse of 10 modulo 17. Check: 10*12=120, 120 mod 17=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Euclidean Algorithm for Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Compute gcd(a, b) using the Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b).
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm computes the greatest common divisor (gcd) of two integers a and b. It is based on the principle that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Intuition: Any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b (since a mod b = a - b*floor(a/b)). By repeatedly replacing (a, b) with (b, a mod b), the numbers decrease until b=0, at which point gcd = a. Algorithm: While b ≠ 0: (a, b) = (b, a % b). Return a. Time complexity is O(log min(a, b)) because each step reduces the numbers significantly (at least by factor ~1.618, golden ratio). Space: O(1) iterative. Connection: GCD computation is the foundation of EEA (L1), as EEA extends this to find coefficients. Common mistakes: Not handling negative inputs (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating correctly. Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Compute gcd(48,18): Step1: a=48, b=18. Since b≠0, compute r = 48 % 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. Step2: b≠0, r = 18 % 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. Step3: b≠0, r = 12 % 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. Loop ends as b=0. Return a=6. Check: divisors of 48: 1,2,3,4,6,8,12,16,24,48; of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18; common: 1,2,3,6; max=6.

Compute gcd(35,15): Step1: a=35, b=15, r=35%15=5. Update: a=15, b=5. Step2: b≠0, r=15%5=0. Update: a=5, b=0. Loop ends. Return a=5. Check: divisors of 35: 1,5,7,35; of 15: 1,3,5,15; common: 1,5; max=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo (Remainder)
ladders.desc: Compute quotient q = a // b and remainder r = a % b such that a = b*q + r.
ladders.explanation: Integer division and modulo are fundamental operations. For integers a (dividend) and b (divisor, b ≠ 0), integer division yields quotient q = floor(a / b) (in Python, a // b), and modulo yields remainder r = a % b, satisfying the division algorithm: a = b*q + r, where 0 ≤ r < |b|. Intuition: This breaks a into multiples of b plus a remainder. Modulo gives the remainder after division, crucial for modular arithmetic. Algorithm: Direct computation using language operators. Time: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1). Connection: This operation is used repeatedly in Euclidean algorithm (L3) to compute a mod b. Common mistakes: Confusing integer division with float division, misunderstanding negative operands (in Python, remainder r has same sign as divisor b, and q rounds toward negative infinity). Real code pattern: q = a // b; r = a % b; verify: a == b*q + r.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Coprime Numbers and Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Two numbers are coprime if their gcd is 1. Modular arithmetic deals with remainders modulo n.
ladders.explanation: Coprime numbers (or relatively prime) are integers a and b such that gcd(a, b) = 1. This means they share no common positive factors other than 1. For example, 8 and 15 are coprime because factors of 8: 1,2,4,8; of 15: 1,3,5,15; only common is 1. Modular arithmetic is arithmetic on integers where numbers 'wrap around' after reaching a modulus n. We say a ≡ b (mod n) if n divides (a - b). The set {0,1,...,n-1} forms a ring modulo n. Key operations: (a + b) mod n, (a * b) mod n. The modular inverse of a modulo n exists if and only if a and n are coprime. Intuition: If a and n share factors, multiplying a by any integer will always yield a multiple of that factor modulo n, never 1. Time: Checking coprimality via gcd is O(log min(a, b)). Space: O(1). Connection: This foundation ensures that for modular inverse to exist in L0, a and p must be coprime. Common mistakes: Assuming inverse exists for all a (only if coprime), confusing modulo with remainder. Real code pattern: def are_coprime(a, b): return gcd(a, b) == 1.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(8,15): Factors of 8: 1,2,4,8. Factors of 15: 1,3,5,15. No common factors except 1. Or use Euclidean algorithm: 15 % 8 = 7, 8 % 7 = 1, 7 % 1 = 0, gcd=1. Since gcd=1, they are coprime.
7 + 12 = 19. 19 mod 5: Divide 19 by 5, quotient 3, remainder 4 because 19 = 5*3 + 4. Alternatively, compute each mod: 7 mod 5 = 2, 12 mod 5 = 2, then (2+2) mod 5 = 4.
